questions.text: I build review cycles into my work to catch errors before they compound.
I believe that the capacity for dispassionate analysis in the face of human tragedy is indispensable for systems designed to serve large populations.
I tend to search for important documents when I need them rather than filing them systematically.
I believe that objective assessment of emotional situations is the competence that most clearly distinguishes thoughtful intervention from reactive involvement.
I feel comfortable operating without a clearly designated leader.
I find that the moral pull of suffering has been the defining influence on the choices that matter most in my life.
I rarely feel the kind of emotional urgency that demands immediate action.
I find that I know things about people's inner states that they have not explicitly shared.
I hold my commitments loosely, knowing that priorities shift and flexibility matters.
I find that my internal experience is often far more intense than what shows on my face.
I believe that what I do for others outlasts anything I do for myself.
I tend to evaluate opportunities primarily by how much they help me reach my goals.
I do not spend much time analyzing the subtext of conversations.
I routinely advocate for my own interests in negotiations and discussions.
I tend to receive compliments and praise with a sense of quiet satisfaction rather than elation.
I am comfortable standing alone in my position if I think it is right.
I feel that being in a supportive role allows me to do my best work.
I hold the view that the simplest interpretation of someone's words is usually the correct one.
